interview_name: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith

main_summary.summary: Joint oral history with Raylawni G. Branch (b. September 1, 1941, Hattiesburg) and Jeanette Smith (b. January 1, 1940, Jones County), recorded by Emilye Crosby on December 1, 2015 at the University of Southern Mississippi Center for Oral History and Cultural Heritage. Both women were Hattiesburg-area NAACP activists; Branch and Gwendolyn Elaine Armstrong were the first Black students to enroll at the University of Southern Mississippi in September 1965. They trace mixed-race ancestry to Captain Newton Knight's Free State of Jones; Branch's grandfather Lee Val Graves attempted to register with registrar Theron Lynd before Branch herself registered in 1961, escorted by JC Fairley, James Bourne, and Vernon Dahmer. They describe Clyde Kennard's framing on chicken-feed-theft charges through false testimony from Johnny Lee Roberts, William D. McCain's role in blocking his USM admission, and Jerry Mitchell's later investigation that produced Roberts's recanting. Smith details her husband Dr. Charles E. Smith's litigation using federal Hill-Burton and Medicare leverage to integrate Forrest General Hospital and the Hattiesburg Clinic, and the Branch household's role in sending Hattiesburg children by Greyhound bus to Washington to compel Head Start funding.
main_summary.historical_significance:
This interview is one of the few first-person accounts pairing the two surviving Black women who integrated the University of Southern Mississippi with a contemporary Hattiesburg organizer from the same NAACP circle. Branch's testimony on Clyde Kennard, including the post-Jerry-Mitchell-investigation conversation in which Johnny Lee Roberts admitted his trial testimony was coached, supplements the documentary record of how the framing operated. Smith's account of her husband Dr. Charles E. Smith's hospital-integration litigation establishes the local mechanics of how the 1946 Hill-Burton Act and the 1965 Medicare provisions translated into compelled compliance at Forrest General Hospital (now Merit Health Wesley) and the Hattiesburg Clinic.

The interview also preserves Branch's Newton-Knight-descended family memory of Free State of Jones, including her great-great-grandmother Rachel and her grandfather Lee Val Graves's documented attempt to register to vote at Theron Lynd's counter, which she discovered in NAACP literature distributed from Washington. Branch's named NAACP escorts during her own 1961 registration (JC Fairley, James Bourne, Vernon Dahmer) and her identification of Mrs. Marjorie James as the Rowan High School teacher who prepared her on the Mississippi Constitution give locally-attested names to the human infrastructure of voter-registration support in Forrest County before Freedom Summer.

chapters.title: Introductions and Free State of Jones
chapters.summary: Emilye Crosby opens the December 1, 2015 interview at the USM Center for Oral History and Cultural Heritage. Smith introduces herself as born January 1, 1940 in Jones County, Mississippi, and recounts her great-great-grandmother Rachel's relationship with Captain Newton Knight, Knight's desertion from the Confederacy, and Dr. Victoria E. Bynum's documentation of the Free State of Jones lineage.
related_events: Civil War desertion from the Confederacy
Publication of Victoria Bynum's Free State of Jones

chapters.title: Branch's Hattiesburg Origins
chapters.summary: Branch describes her birth at 711 Whitney Street in her grandmother's three-room shotgun house, the Hattiesburg neighborhood that included Miss Emma Campbell and Mr. Kelly, and her family's land out around Prentiss, Mississippi at Mount Carmel. She recounts her great-grandparents purchasing 80 acres outright, disputing the 'forty acres and a mule' narrative.
related_events: Post-Reconstruction Black land ownership in Jefferson Davis County

chapters.title: Chicago Migration and Emmett Till
chapters.summary: Branch describes her father's blood disorder and the family's loss of two Chicago homes, their period of homelessness, and her mother's firing from Brock Candy Company after lunching with a Black co-worker. She recounts seeing the Jet magazine photograph of Emmett Till in his casket as her awakening to the depth of racial violence.

chapters.title: Cajun and Mixed-Race Lineage
chapters.summary: Branch traces her maternal grandmother Phanimae Holloway's flight from Prentiss after a rape by a plantation owner, settlement in Hattiesburg, and marriage to a Cajun named Frank Elsie from Southern Louisiana. She describes the 'one drop' classification system and the social ostracism faced by her mother Alpharenzia from both Black and white communities.

chapters.title: Newton Knight Descendants and Protection
chapters.summary: Branch recounts how her grandfather Colonel Robert Hall, a green-eyed and blonde Newton Knight descendant, was permitted by Jim Crow custom to sell guns and ammunition that Black storeowners could not. She describes her grandfather wearing Klan sheets to infiltrate a Soso meeting and force the Klan to leave Jones County alone, and the protective recognition Newton Knight's descendants received from white kin.
related_events: Ku Klux Klan terrorism in Jones and Forrest counties

chapters.title: Integrating USM in 1965
chapters.summary: Branch describes enrolling at the University of Southern Mississippi in September 1965 with Gwendolyn Elaine Armstrong, both Rowan High School graduates. She names Vernon Dahmer, James Bourne, and Elaine Armstrong as key supporters, discusses President William D. McCain's John Birch Society membership, and contrasts the calm of USM's integration with James Meredith's experience at Ole Miss in 1962. The dormitory mirror incident with white students watching for her 'tail' is recounted.
related_events: Ole Miss integration with James Meredith (1962)

chapters.title: Clyde Kennard's Framing
chapters.summary: Branch and Smith give detailed accounts of Clyde Kennard's three attempts to enroll at Mississippi Southern College, the back-channel negotiations between McCain, Governor Ross Barnett, and Black Hattiesburg principals seeking a separate junior college, and the chicken-feed frame-up using the false testimony of nineteen-year-old Johnny Lee Roberts. Branch describes flying to Chicago with Mr. JC Fairley, Dick Gregory, and Charles Evers to pressure Roberts to recant, and Roberts's eventual recanting through Jerry Mitchell of the Clarion-Ledger.
related_events: Clyde Kennard's framing and Parchman Farm imprisonment

chapters.title: Local NAACP and Outside Volunteers
chapters.summary: Smith situates Hattiesburg as a 1964 Freedom Summer destination because of its long-standing NAACP branch (chartered 1946) and the 1950s voter-registration suit. Both women critique outside organizers and writers who took credit for local Black activism, and Branch quotes a Pennsylvania judge: 'If it's not local, it's not real.' Branch describes her segregation-era jobs as the first Black hire at the Hattiesburg telephone company and Sears, and the Big Yank garment factory permanent-press shift.

chapters.title: Hospital Integration and Bill Thacker
chapters.summary: Smith describes her husband Dr. Charles E. Smith building a clinic in Hattiesburg, the Forrest General Hospital (then called Methodist Hospital, now Merit Health Wesley) practice of consigning Black patients to a single floor that is now a cafeteria hallway, and the basement charity-ward in Laurel. She describes Dr. Bill Thacker, a white Hattiesburg dentist who treated Black patients despite Klan threats to kidnap his daughters, and the brick wall he built around his house.

chapters.title: Voter Registration with Theron Lynd
chapters.summary: Branch describes registering to vote at age twenty-one in 1961 at the counter of Forrest County registrar Theron Lynd, escorted by Mr. JC Fairley, Mr. James Bourne, and Mr. Vernon Dahmer. She credits Rowan High School world history teacher Mrs. Marjorie James for her preparation on the Mississippi Constitution. She also recounts learning from an NAACP poster distributed from Washington that her grandfather Lee Val Graves had attempted to register at Lynd's counter years before.
related_events: Forrest County voter-registration suits of the 1950s

chapters.title: Head Start and Federal Leverage
chapters.summary: Branch recounts her husband's integration of Hattiesburg's medical infrastructure using Hill-Burton and Medicare compliance leverage, his eventual presidency of the local NAACP, and her own conversation with Robert Beach that produced the Greyhound-bus tactic of sending five hundred Hattiesburg children to Washington to compel Head Start funding. The chapter closes with Branch's recollection of meeting her husband at her father's church-dedication in Laurel and his subsequent treatment of her father for pneumonia.
